title: Coq au Vin

overview.description: Chicken slowly braised in red wine with lardons, mushrooms, pearl onions and aromatic herbs.

method: Pat the chicken dry and season lightly with salt and black pepper.
Heat a large heavy-based casserole over a medium heat.
Add the lardons and cook until the fat has rendered and the lardons are golden.
Remove the lardons and set aside, leaving the rendered fat in the casserole.
Add the oil if necessary.
Brown the chicken in batches until golden on all sides.
Remove the chicken and set aside.
Add the carrots and onion to the casserole.
Cook until softened and lightly browned.
Add the garlic and cook for 1 minute.
Sprinkle over the flour and stir well.
Cook for 1 minute.
Gradually pour in the red wine, scraping the bottom of the casserole to release the browned bits.
Add the chicken stock, bay leaves and thyme.
Return the chicken and lardons to the casserole.
Bring to a gentle simmer.
Cover and cook gently for approximately 1 hour, or until the chicken is tender.
Meanwhile, melt the butter in a frying pan.
Cook the mushrooms until golden.
Add the pearl onions and cook until lightly browned and tender.
Add the mushrooms and pearl onions to the casserole for the final 20 minutes of cooking.
Remove the bay leaves and thyme stems.
Taste and adjust the seasoning.
Scatter with fresh parsley before serving.

learning.did-you-know: Coq au vin literally means 'rooster with wine'. The dish is traditionally associated with Burgundy, although many French regions have their own versions using local wines and ingredients.
learning.what-you-will-learn: How to brown meat, build a wine-based braising sauce and slowly cook chicken until tender and deeply flavoured.

notes.why-its-in-the-chapman-kitchen: A classic French dish that teaches how time, wine and gentle cooking can transform inexpensive chicken into a deeply flavoured meal.
chapman-tips: Dry the chicken thoroughly before browning so it colours rather than steams.
Brown the chicken in batches to avoid overcrowding the casserole.
Don't skip deglazing the pan; the browned bits are a major source of flavour.
Cook the mushrooms separately so they brown rather than becoming watery in the braising liquid.
The dish is often even better the following day after the flavours have had time to develop.
notes.batch-cooking: Cool completely before freezing in portions. Freeze the chicken and sauce together.
notes.leftovers: Reheat gently and serve with mashed potatoes, noodles or crusty bread. The chicken can also be removed from the bones and used in a pie or pasta dish.
